by_judge.judge: SACHIN SHANKAR MAGADUM & RAJESH RAI K
S VISHWAJITH SHETTY & RAJESH RAI K

narrative:
On 7 May 2026, the Karnataka High Court heard 170 of 235 scheduled matters, for an overall hearing rate of **72.3%**. That is below the **81.9%** recorded on 5 May, though today’s list was much larger at 235 matters versus 116. Relative to the broader, heavier boards seen on 30 April (**44.3%**) and 28 April (**40.3%**), today’s disposal pace was substantially stronger.

Judge-wise, the strongest substantial performance came from **Sachin Shankar Magadum & Rajesh Rai K**, who heard **35 of 43 matters (81.4%)**, the best rate among benches with meaningful volume. One **S Vishwajith Shetty** board also performed strongly at **56 of 70 (80.0%)**, while his other 41-case board cleared **27 (65.9%)**. **V Srishananda** heard **49 of 76 (64.5%)**, handling the day’s largest single load but at a more moderate conversion rate. The single-case **S Vishwajith Shetty & Rajesh Rai K** board was technically at **100%**, but on negligible volume. Compared with 5 May, both the Magadum-Rajesh Rai K bench and the main Shetty board were somewhat less efficient, though both still remained among the better-performing boards.

Stage-wise, **ORDERS** was the clearest conversion category, with **53 of 63 matters heard (84.1%)**, outperforming **PRELIMINARY HEARING** at **70.8%**, **ADMISSION** at **66.7%**, and **UNKNOWN** at **65.5%**. That continues the pattern from 5 May, when Orders was also the best-converting major stage at **93.3%**.

Case-type distribution was led by **WP** matters, which made up **120 of 235 listings** and delivered **90 hearings (75.0%)**, giving the largest contribution to total clearance. **CRL.P** formed the second-largest block at **60 scheduled**, but converted less efficiently at **63.3%**, leaving the biggest residual backlog in absolute terms. Smaller categories such as **MFA (90.0%)**, **WA (80.0%)**, and **COMAP (83.3%)** converted well, while **CRL.RP** remained weak at **50.0%**, broadly in line with its modest conversion pattern on 5 May (**53.8%**).
